title: Database Migration Strategy
description: Best practices for database schema migrations
content:
## Database Migration Best Practices

### Principles
1. Always use version-controlled migration scripts
2. Test migrations on staging first
3. Include rollback scripts
4. Never modify existing migrations

### Example (PostgreSQL)
```sql
-- Migration: Add user roles
-- Version: 003

CREATE TABLE user_roles (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id INTEGER REFERENCES users(id),
  role VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
);

-- Rollback
DROP TABLE user_roles;
```

### Tools
- Django migrations
- Rails migrations
- Fly

title: Nginx Load Balancer Configuration
description: Setting up Nginx as a load balancer for web applications

title: Logging Configuration Guide
description: Setting up structured logging for applications
content:
## Structured Logging Setup

### JSON Logging Format
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-06-20T12:00:00Z",
  "level": "INFO",
  "message": "User logged in",
  "context": {
    "user_id": 123,
    "ip": "192.168.1.1"
  }
}
```

### Log Levels
- DEBUG: Detailed debugging
- INFO: General information
- WARN: Warning conditions
- ERROR: Error conditions
- FATAL: Critical failures

### Aggregation Tools
- ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana)
- Loki + Grafana
- CloudWatch Logs

Related: [[atoms/metho

title: Kubernetes Cluster Setup
description: Setting up a local or production Kubernetes cluster
content:
## Kubernetes Setup Guide

### Local Development (Minikube)
```bash
# Install minikube
brew install minikube

# Start cluster
minikube start

# Verify
kubectl cluster-info
```

### Production Setup
Use managed services:
- AWS EKS
- Google GKE
- Azure AKS

Related: [[atoms/definitions/kubernetes]], [[atoms/methods/kubernetes-deployment-yaml]]

title: Nextcloud Deployment Guide
description: Complete guide for deploying Nextcloud self-hosted cloud
content:
## Nextcloud Deployment

### Quick Install (Docker)
```bash
docker run -d \
  -p 8080:80 \
  -v nextcloud_data:/var/www/html \
  nextcloud
```

### Production Setup
1. Install prerequisites (PHP, MySQL/MariaDB)
2. Download Nextcloud archive
3. Configure web server (Nginx/Apache)
4. Run installer
5. Configure SSL ([[atoms/methods/ssl-certificate-setup]])

### Integration
- LDAP authentication
- Collabora Online (document editing)
- Nextcloud Talk (video calls)

Related: [[atoms/definitions/web-ap

title: API Design Best Practices
description: Guidelines for designing RESTful APIs
content:
## REST API Design Guidelines

### URL Structure
- Use nouns: `/users`, `/orders`
- Use plural convention
- Hierarchical: `/users/{id}/orders`

### HTTP Methods
- GET: Read (no body modification)
- POST: Create
- PUT/PATCH: Update
- DELETE: Remove

### Response Codes
- 200: Success
- 201: Created
- 400: Bad request
- 404: Not found
- 500: Server error

### Versioning
- URL: `/api/v1/users`
- Header: `Accept: application/vnd.api.v1+json`

Related: [[atoms/definitions/rest-api]], [[atoms/facts/api

title: Kubernetes Deployment YAML
description: Creating deployment manifests for Kubernetes
content:
## Kubernetes Deployment Manifest

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: myapp-deployment
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: myapp
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: myapp
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: myapp
        image: myapp:v1
        ports:
        - containerPort: 3000
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: myapp-service
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 3000
  select

title: SSL Certificate Setup with Let's Encrypt
description: Setting up free SSL certificates using Certbot
content:
## SSL Certificate Setup

### Using Certbot
```bash
# Install certbot
sudo apt install certbot python3-certbot-nginx

# Obtain certificate
sudo certbot --nginx -d example.com -d www.example.com

# Auto-renewal
sudo certbot renew --dry-run
```

### Nginx SSL Config
```nginx
server {
  listen 443 ssl http2;
  ssl_certificate /etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com/fullchain.pem;
  ssl_certificate_key /etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com/privkey.pem;
  
  ssl_protocols TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3;
  ssl_ciphers ECDHE-E

title: Docker Compose Configuration
description: Multi-container application configuration with Docker Compose
content:
## Docker Compose Setup

### Sample compose.yaml
```yaml
version: '3.8'
services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    depends_on:
      - db
  db:
    image: postgres:15
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret
    volumes:
      - db_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data

volumes:
  db_data:
```

### Commands
- `docker compose up -d` - Start services
- `docker compose down` - Stop services
- `docker compose logs` - View logs

Related: [[atoms/methods/docker-container-setup]],

title: CI/CD Pipeline Setup
description: Setting up continuous integration and deployment pipelines
content:
## CI/CD Pipeline with GitHub Actions

### Workflow Example
```yaml
name: Deploy Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - run: npm install
      - run: npm test
  
  deploy:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - run: docker build -t app:v1 .
      - run: docker push registry/app:v1
```

### Stages
1. Build
2. Test
3. Security scan
4. Deploy to staging
5. Deploy to production

title: Docker Container Setup
description: Steps to create and run Docker containers for applications
content:
## Docker Container Setup Guide

### Prerequisites
- Install Docker Engine
- Verify installation: `docker --version`

### Steps

1. **Create Dockerfile**
   ```dockerfile
   FROM node:18-alpine
   WORKDIR /app
   COPY package*.json ./
   RUN npm install
   COPY . .
   CMD ["npm", "start"]
   ```

2. **Build Image**
   ```bash
   docker build -t myapp:v1 .
   ```

3. **Run Container**
   ```bash
   docker run -d -p 3000:3000 myapp:v1
   ```

Related: [[atoms/definitions/containerization]], [[atom

title: Nginx Reverse Proxy Setup
description: Configuring Nginx as a reverse proxy server

title: OAuth Integration Guide
description: Implementing OAuth 2.0 authentication in applications
content:
## OAuth 2.0 Integration

### Flow Types
- Authorization Code (most secure)
- Implicit (deprecated)
- Client Credentials (machine-to-machine)
- Refresh Token (renew access)

### Implementation Steps
1. Register application with provider
2. Configure redirect URI
3. Implement authorization endpoint
4. Handle token exchange
5. Validate tokens

### Example: Google OAuth
```javascript
const oauth2Client = new OAuth2Client(
  CLIENT_ID,
  CLIENT_SECRET,
  REDIRECT_URI
);
```

Related: [[atoms/definit

title: Application Monitoring Setup
description: Setting up Prometheus and Grafana for application monitoring
content:
## Monitoring Stack Setup

### Prometheus Configuration
```yaml
# prometheus.yml
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s

scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'app'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['localhost:9090']
  
  - job_name: 'node'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['localhost:9100']
```

### Grafana Dashboards
- Import standard dashboards
- Create custom visualizations
- Set up alerting rules

Key metrics: CPU, memory, request latency, error rate

Related: [[atoms/facts/prometheus-metrics]], [[

title: Log Aggregation Benefits
description: Advantages of centralized log collection
content:
Log Aggregation Benefits:

- Centralized troubleshooting
- Correlation across services
- Historical analysis
- Compliance requirements
- Anomaly detection
- Performance insights

Popular stacks:
- ELK (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana)
- EFK (Elasticsearch, Fluentd, Kibana)
- PLG (Prometheus, Loki, Grafana)

Related: [[atoms/methods/logging-configuration]], [[atoms/methods/monitoring-setup]]

title: HTTPS Security Benefits
description: Security advantages of HTTPS over HTTP
content:
HTTPS Security Benefits:

- Data encryption in transit
- Identity verification (certificate)
- Prevents MITM attacks
- Protects against eavesdropping
- Required for HTTP/2
- SEO ranking boost

TLS 1.3 improvements:
- Faster handshake
- Better cipher suites
- Forward secrecy default

Related: [[atoms/methods/ssl-certificate-setup]], [[atoms/methods/nginx-reverse-proxy]]

title: Docker Networking Modes
description: Different network configurations available in Docker
content:
Docker Network Types:

- bridge: Default isolated network
- host: Container shares host network
- overlay: Multi-host networking (Swarm)
- macvlan: Container gets MAC address
- none: No network access

Commands:
```bash
docker network create mynet
docker network connect mynet container1
```

Related: [[atoms/methods/docker-compose-configuration]], [[atoms/methods/docker-container-setup]]

title: MongoDB Capabilities
description: Key capabilities of MongoDB document database
content:
MongoDB offers:

- Document-based storage (BSON)
- Horizontal scaling via sharding
- Replica sets for high availability
- Flexible schema
- Aggregation pipeline
- Atlas cloud service

Comparison: [[atoms/facts/postgresql-features]]

Related: [[atoms/definitions/database]]

title: PostgreSQL Key Features
description: Important features of PostgreSQL database system
content:
PostgreSQL provides:

- ACID compliance
- Advanced indexing (B-tree, GiST, GIN)
- Full-text search
- JSON support (JSONB)
- Table inheritance
- Window functions
- Extensions system

Related: [[atoms/definitions/database]], [[atoms/methods/database-migration]]

title: API Rate Limiting Strategies
description: Common rate limiting approaches for APIs
content:
Rate Limiting Algorithms:

- Token bucket: Fixed tokens refill over time
- Sliding window: Smooth request distribution
- Fixed window: Count requests per time window
- Leaky bucket: Constant outflow rate

Implementation:
```nginx
limit_req_zone $binary_remote_addr zone=api:10m rate=10r/s;
limit_req zone=api burst=20 nodelay;
```

Headers: `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`

Related: [[atoms/methods/api-design-best-practices]], [[atoms/definitions/rest-api]]

title: JWT Security Considerations
description: Security best practices for JWT tokens
content:
JWT Security Best Practices:

- Use short expiration times (15-30 minutes)
- Store refresh tokens securely
- Never store JWTs in localStorage (use httpOnly cookies)
- Always validate signature
- Use strong signing algorithm (RS256 > HS256)
- Include only minimal claims

Attack vectors: token theft, replay attacks

Related: [[atoms/definitions/authentication]], [[atoms/methods/oauth-integration]]

title: Prometheus Metric Types
description: Four core metric types in Prometheus
content:
Prometheus Metric Types:

- Counter: Cumulative value (requests_total)
- Gauge: Point-in-time value (memory_usage)
- Histogram: Distribution (request_duration)
- Summary: Similar to histogram with quantiles

Query examples:
```promql
rate(http_requests_total[5m])
avg(node_memory_usage_bytes)
histogram_quantile(0.95, request_duration)
```

Related: [[atoms/methods/monitoring-setup]]

title: Authentication Mechanisms
description: Processes for verifying user identity
content:
**Authentication** verifies who a user is.

Common methods:
- Password-based
- OAuth/OIDC (see [[atoms/methods/oauth-integration]])
- JWT tokens ([[atoms/facts/jwt-security]])
- Multi-factor authentication

Critical for [[atoms/definitions/web-application]] security.

title: Kubernetes
description: Open-source container orchestration platform
content:
**Kubernetes (K8s)** automates deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications.

Core concepts:
- Pods: Smallest deployable units
- Services: Network exposure
- Deployments: Rolling updates
- Built on [[atoms/definitions/containerization]]

See [[atoms/methods/kubernetes-cluster-setup]]

title: Web Application Definition
description: A software application that runs on a web server and is accessed through a browser
content:
A **Web Application** is a client-server software application where the client runs in a web browser.

Common examples include:
- [[atoms/methods/nextcloud-deployment]]
- [[atoms/methods/docker-container-setup]]
- Online banking systems
- Social media platforms

title: Microservices Architecture
description: An architectural style where applications are composed of small, independent services
content:
**Microservices Architecture** breaks applications into small, loosely coupled services.

Key characteristics:
- Each service handles a specific business capability
- Services communicate via APIs (see [[atoms/definitions/rest-api]])
- Independent deployment and scaling
- Often uses [[atoms/definitions/containerization]]

title: Containerization
description: Packaging software with its dependencies into standardized units
content:
**Containerization** encapsulates an application with its environment and dependencies.

Benefits:
- Consistent runtime across environments
- Lightweight compared to VMs
- Easy deployment via [[atoms/methods/docker-container-setup]]
- Foundation for [[atoms/definitions/kubernetes]]

title: Load Balancing
description: Distributing network traffic across multiple servers
content:
**Load Balancing** improves availability and performance.

Strategies:
- Round-robin
- Least connections
- IP hash

Tools: Nginx, HAProxy (see [[atoms/methods/nginx-load-balancer]])

Essential for [[atoms/definitions/microservices]] scaling.

title: REST API
description: Representational State Transfer - a standard for web service communication
content:
**REST API** is an architectural style for designing networked applications.

HTTP methods:
- GET: Retrieve data
- POST: Create new resources
- PUT: Update existing resources
- DELETE: Remove resources

Related: [[atoms/methods/api-design-best-practices]]

title: Database Systems
description: Organized collection of structured information stored electronically
content:
A **Database** stores and manages structured data.

Types:
- Relational (SQL): [[atoms/facts/postgresql-features]]
- NoSQL: [[atoms/facts/mongodb-capabilities]]
- In-memory: Redis
- Graph: Neo4j

Related methods: [[atoms/methods/database-migration]]

title: API Performance Benchmarks
description: Typical response time benchmarks for API endpoints
content:
## API Response Time Benchmarks

| Endpoint Type | Target Latency |
|--------------|---------------|
| Simple GET | < 50ms |
| Complex Query | < 200ms |
| Write Operation | < 100ms |
| Batch Process | < 500ms |

Acceptable error rate: < 0.1%

Related: [[atoms/methods/api-design-best-practices]], [[atoms/facts/api-rate-limiting]]

title: SQL vs NoSQL - Which to Choose?
description: Decision criteria for choosing database type
content:
## Question

When should I use SQL vs NoSQL databases?

### Choose SQL When:
- Data is structured and relational
- Complex queries needed
- ACID compliance critical
- Schema is stable

### Choose NoSQL When:
- Unstructured data
- High write throughput
- Flexible schema needed
- Horizontal scaling priority

Examples: [[atoms/facts/postgresql-features]] vs [[atoms/facts/mongodb-capabilities]]

Related: [[atoms/definitions/database]]

title: OAuth vs JWT - Authentication Choice
description: Choosing between OAuth and JWT for authentication
content:
## Question

Should I use OAuth or JWT for authentication?

### OAuth Advantages:
- Delegated authorization
- Third-party login (Google, GitHub)
- Token refresh built-in
- Enterprise-friendly

### JWT Advantages:
- Self-contained tokens
- Stateless verification
- Simple implementation
- Good for microservices

Often used together: OAuth for login, JWT for API access.

Related: [[atoms/definitions/authentication]], [[atoms/methods/oauth-integration]]

title: When to use Docker vs Kubernetes?
description: Choosing the right containerization tool for different scenarios
content:
## Question

When should I use Docker alone versus Kubernetes?

### Docker Suitable For:
- Single server deployments
- Development environments
- Simple applications
- Quick prototyping

### Kubernetes Suitable For:
- Multi-server clusters
- Production scaling needs
- Complex microservices
- High availability requirements

Related: [[atoms/definitions/containerization]], [[atoms/definitions/kubernetes]]
